data.title: Dollar Better Bid Ahead Of Jobs Report As Japanese And German Data Disappoint, But Will It Be Sustained?
data.description:
Overview

It is not clear what happened yesterday, the first time US and Chinese leaders have spoken since the inauguration. The US readout suggests trade was only discussed, and a deal on the rare earths was reached. China's readout included an expression of concern about the US planned arms sales to Taiwan and the need for more talks to resolve the issue. The agreement in Geneva apparently covered bilateral actions, and Beijing's export controls on several critical minerals and magnets are universal. Still, more talks have been planned, even if not scheduled. Although the Trump-Musk break-up is as dramatic as one might expect, given the volatile personalities, the focus is on today's US employment data after a series of disappointing reports, including ADP and the weekly jobless claims. Still, the greenback is firmer against the G10 currencies, but mixed against the emerging market currencies, where the euro's pullback is a drag on central European currencies.

Equities were mixed in the Asia Pacific region. South Korea's Kospi was the strongest with a 1.5% gain and a 5.3% rise on the week as election offers a chance for political stability after the turmoil earlier this year. Europe's Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is flat as it tries to extend its advance for the fourth consecutive session. US index futures are 035%-0.50% higher as they recoup part of yesterday's losses. Bond yields are softer. Poor household spending data from Japan weighed on rate, and even at the very long-end of the curve. Disappointing Germany industrial production and export figures helped drag European bond yields 2-4 bps lower. The 10-year US Treasury yield (US10Y) is near 4.37%, slightly lower on the day and a little more than six basis points lower on the week, rivalling the 10-year Gilt for the best performance this week. Gold is consolidating quietly within yesterday's range and is trading so far today mostly between $3353 and $3375. It settled slightly below $3290 last week. July WTI remains in the upper end of Monday's trading range, which extended to almost $64. It settled on Monday near $62.50 and has mostly held above it. Today's range is roughly $62.80-$63.35.

USD: The Dollar Index is recovering from yesterday's decline, which brought it to around 98.35, its lowest level since April 22. It was initially sold in the response to ECB's Lagarde's observation, which the swaps market had already concluded, that the ECB easing cycle is nearly over. However, news that Trump and Xi spoke and seemed to have overcome what appears to be a misunderstanding lifted DXY to around 98.85, a little above Wednesday's low (~98.65). Follow-through buying today is probing the 99.00 area. It may take a move above the 99.40 area to lift the technical tone. Barring a new social media post by the president, the US employment data is the key ahead of the weekend. The consensus sees slower job growth in May. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey is for 130k increase after a 177k rise April. The average through the first four months of the year is 144k, down from 176k average in the same period last year. The market may be more sensitive to a change in the unemployment rate, which stood at 4.2% in both March and April. It was at 4.0% last May. The JOLTS report (April) showed an unexpected rise in job openings, led by the private sector, while openings in manufacturing and leisure and hospitality fell. On the other hand, the ADP private sector estimate for May was dismal at 37k, a third of what was expected). It was the lowest estimate since the fluke -53k loss in March 2023. Although the short-term divergence between the BLS estimate and ADP can be stark, what some news wires call the "whisper number" is bound to be lower. A weak report today, and especially a rise in the unemployment rate, could see the market push the next Fed cut back into Q3. Alongside the gradual slowing of the labor market, and several elevated measures of consumer debt stress, consumer credit slowed in Q1 25 (to just less than $20 bln) from more than $30 bln in Q4 24 and $25 bln in Q1 24. April's figures will be released today, but typically do not move the markets.

Euro: The "hawkish cut" by the ECB lifted the euro to almost $1.1500 yesterday from a low on Wednesday around $1.1360. On the pullback on Trump-Xi talks, the single currency found new bids near $1.1430. It has pulled back to almost $1.1410 today, partly in response to disappointing German data. Support extends toward $1.1380. Although April factory orders were yesterday rose by 0.6% instead of a decline by 1.5% as economists polled by Bloomberg expected, industrial production fell by more than forecast (-1.4% vs. -1.0%) and the March series was revised to show a 2.3% increase instead of 3.0%. And April exports fell by 1.7%, which was also more than anticipated. Yet, on the eurozone level, growth in Q1 was twice as strong as initially reported (0.6% vs. 0.3%). The swaps market went into the meeting anticipating a year-end rate slightly above 1.60% and rose eight basis points by the end of the session.

CNY: The dollar held above the seven-month low against the offshore yuan seen in late May near CNH7.1615) and recovered to about CNH7.1750 before stalling yesterday. Today, it has taken out yesterday's high (~CNH7.1820) and risen to about CNH7.1870. The price action over the past few weeks may be carving out a new range between CNH7.16 and CNH7.23. The PBOC set the dollar's reference rate at CNY7.1845 (CNY7.1865 yesterday and CNY7.1848 last Friday). China reports May CPI and PPI early Monday. The market expected deflationary forces still grip prices. The median forecast in Bloomberg's survey sees consumer prices falling by 0.2% year-over-year after falling 0.1% in April. Consumer prices turned down in February after having been slowly rising since January 2024. Although many focus on weak demand (though presented as a percentage of GDP, which, we argue, reflects continued strong investment) the drag on goods prices comes from food. Core prices, excluding food and energy, has been mostly positive, though they did slip in February below zero. It stood at 0.5% in April year-over-year. Note that earlier this week, Switzerland reported that on the harmonized EU methodology, its CPI is -0.2% year-over-year. However, the Swiss 10-year yield is around 0.25%, while China's is hovering near 1.70%. China's producer prices are expected to have fallen 3.0% year-over-year after a 2.7% decline in April. If so, that would match the deepest deflation since July 2023 and the third consecutive decline. It had appeared to be stabilizing in the previous four months.

JPY: The dollar has chopped back and forth between around JPY142.40 and JPY144.40 this week. It is making session highs in the European morning near JPY144.20. Still, it may require a close above JPY144.80 to signal a breakout. Japan's household spending was a major disappointment. Rather than rise 1.5% as the economists projected, it fell by 0.1%. The average year-over-year gain in Q1 of 0.8% was the best quarterly performance since Q3 22. Yet in GDP terms, consumer spending rose by 0.2% in Q1 25 after a 0.3% increase in Q4 24. The Q1 GDP estimate of a 0.2% contraction quarter-over-quarter will be updated early Monday.

GBP: Sterling rose to a new three-year high yesterday near $1.3615. It held in better than the euro, and on the pullback to only around $1.3575 bids re-emerged. It is holding below $1.3600 today, and tested support in the $1.3530 area in the European morning. A break of it could see $1.3490-$1.3500. A close above $1.3575-80 would appear constructive. The UK-US trade deal seemed to allow British steel and aluminum into the US without the levy the US re-introduced, provided that its supply chain was clean (i.e., not re-exporting Chinese product). Yet, it turns out that UK steel and aluminum will be subject to the 25% tariff announced last week after the US agreed to allow US Steel to be combined with Nippon Steel. The UK also seems vulnerable if the final version of the US budget proposal contains Section 899, which retaliates against companies operating in the US whose home country pursues policies that the US does not like, such as a digital tax or implementing the OCED's Pillar Two, (sets15% minimum corporate tax rate on large companies and allows others to top it off if it is under-taxed at home).

CAD: The Canadian dollar made a new high for the year yesterday after Ottawa reported a record-goods trade deficit as exports to the US collapsed by almost 16%. The greenback reached CAD1.3635 before recovering toward CAD1.3675. It has extended the recovered to almost around CAD1.3685, but the CAD1.3700 area looks more important. A move above there could target CAD1.3750. After the Bank of Canada's pause on Wednesday, with overnight target rate at 2.75%, which is within estimates of the neutral range, attention turns to the labor market today. Canada's labor market has slowed considerably in recent months. The average monthly jobs growth in the first four months of 2025 is about 13k, down from almost 42k a month in the Jan-Apr 2024 period. The median forecast tin Bloomberg's survey is for a 12.5k loss of jobs last month. Canada has lost an average of 3.75k full time positions a month this year, and grew 25k a month in the same period last year. The unemployment rate has been steadily rising since bottoming at 5% in late 2022 and again in early 2023. It was at 5.7% in January 2024 and finished the year at 6.7%. Canada's unemployment rate stood at 6.9% in April, matching the cyclical high. It is expected to have risen to 7.0% last month.

AUD: According to Bloomberg's pricing, the Australian dollar made a new seven-month high yesterday by 1/100 of a cent to nearly $0.6540.The technical objective we have been emphasizing was the (61.8%) retracement of the Australian dollar's decline from last October's high (~$0.6940) to April's low (~$0.5915) found near $0.6550. It settled above $0.6500 for the first time this year, but was unable to sustain the momentum and has pulled back to about $0.6485 today. A band of support may be found between $0.6455 and $0.6475.

MXN: The dollar reached a new eight-month low against the peso today, slightly below MXN19.14. The MXN19.00-MXN19.05 offers the next important chart area. It has been a gradual but persistent grind lower in recent weeks. And that is the point. The relatively low volatility, liquidity, and attractive rates make it an ideal for carry-trades against the dollar. Its actual volatility over the past month is around 8.9% (annualized). One can earn more carry in Brazil, but the volatility is almost 50% higher than the peso, and the liquidity is worse. One of the implications of this is that a firm CPI print on Monday, with both the headline and core rates moving above the top of the target range that extends to 4%, the peso's attractiveness may be enhanced rather than diminished.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CoreWeave: Benefiting From AI Workload Growth; Initiate With 'Buy'
data.description:
CoreWeave (CRWV) is an AI Hyperscaler, offering proprietary software and cloud services for enterprise customers through its CoreWeave Cloud Platform. I like the company’s unique value proposition in the rapidly growing AI hyperscaler market. I am initiating with a ‘Buy’ rating with a fair value of $191 per share.

I think CoreWeave’s competitive advantages can be summarized as follows:

  • As disclosed in its IPO prospectus, the company operated 32 data centers running with more than 250,000 GPUs as of December 31, 2024. I think these active purpose-built data centers could enable the company to serve a wide range of enterprise customers, providing essential AI workloads and computing power for everything from training to inference use cases. It is relatively easy for enterprise customers to establish long-term contracts with CoreWeave for AI computing capabilities, compared to DIY.
  • As depicted in the slide below, CoreWeave offers a holistic suite of solutions for AI training and inference computing needs, including cloud ops, lifecycle management, observability, networking and storage solutions on top of its data center infrastructure layer. Over the past few years, CoreWeave has built robust relationships with key industry players in the observability, security, storage, chips as well as networking industries, which could enable CoreWeave to build a comprehensive AI hyperscaler environment that integrates both proprietary technologies as well as best-in-class third-party services and solutions.

  • Typically, enterprises need multiple years to fully integrate and utilize CoreWeave's AI infrastructure, as detailed in the slide below. These multi-year contracts require ongoing technology support from CoreWeave and involve tremendous data and workload migrations, making CoreWeave’s business highly sticky. Due to high migration costs and extended timeline, it would be hard for CoreWeave’s customers to terminate their long-term contracts. In other words, CoreWeave’s business is highly recurring in nature. As the company disclosed, long-term contracts represent around 96% of total revenue, which is quite remarkable.

  • I think CoreWeave has a huge total addressable markets [TAM], and in the IPO prospectus, the company estimated their total TAM for AI inference/fine-tuning, AI workload monitoring, and training infrastructure will grow at a CAGR of 38% from 2023 to 2028, reaching around $330 billion related to training infrastructure. Indeed, CoreWeave has a huge runway for future growth, driven by market expansion fueled by AI workloads, assuming the company continues to expand their data center presents and integrates more solutions and software into their CoreWeave Cloud Platform.
  • Lastly, the management plans to expand their AI cloud platform into some new industries, including highly regulated sectors such as banks and healthcare industries. It is evident that technology and communication services companies are the early adopters of AI cloud platform, and I think traditional industries are gradually migrating their workloads to AI cloud infrastructure. Therefore, CoreWeave has huge potentials to expand its cloud infrastructure into these highly regulated and traditional industries, which could potentially accelerate its business growth.

CoreWeave released its Q1 FY25 result on May 14, posting 420% revenue growth and 550% adjusted operating income growth. As depicted in the chart below, CoreWeave has experienced explosive business growth over the past few quarters driven by new customers wins and rapid growth of AI workloads.

My key takeaway from the quarter was the $11.9 billion contract signed with OpenAI in March 2025. OpenAI will acquire $350.0 million of CoreWeave stock through a new issuance. I think the partnership with OpenAI validates that CoreWeave’s AI ability to satisfy the complex demands of advanced AI machine learning. I have no doubt that CoreWeave’s technology and platform could be potentially adopted by large enterprises customers in the future. During the Q1 FY25 earnings call, the management disclosed a recent $4 billion contract with another large AI enterprise. I anticipate CoreWeave will continue to win more deals in the near future.

For FY25, the company is guiding for $4.9-$5 billion in revenue with $800-$830 million in adjusted operating income, as detailed in the slide below.

Despite CoreWeave’s explosive growth, the company is only anticipated to achieve around $5 billion in revenue for FY25. As discussed previously, their multi-year contracts have a long lead time from initial agreement to the full implementation. As such, I think CoreWeave is in the infant stage of business expansion. I forecast the company’s revenue will grow by 165% in FY25, aligned with the company’s guidance. Due to the long-term nature of their contracts, I think the company has great visibilities for near-term revenue growth. I anticipate the revenue growth will slow down to 80% in FY26, then decelerate to 50% from FY27 to FY28, followed by 30% normalized revenue growth from FY29 onwards. If the overall AI training infrastructure reaches $330 billion in FY28, as the management anticipated, I calculate CoreWeave’s revenue will only represent slightly over 5% of the total market by FY28, which I consider quite reasonable.

I anticipate 10bps annual margin expansion. As the company is investing heavily in CAPEX, I forecast their depreciation costs will grow significantly over the next few years, which will cause challenges for their margin expansion. I calculate the rising depreciation costs will bring 30bps operating margin pressures for the company, while the reduction in SG&A expenses will generate 40bps of operating leverage. All in all, I forecast the company will deliver 10bps annual operating margin expansion.

DCF summary:

FCFF:

As the company generates negative free cash flow, I anticipate the company will borrow $15 billion of new debts to fund its business growth in the next five years. WACC is calculated to be 10.1% assuming: risk-free 4.2%; beta 1.3; equity risk premium 6%; cost of debt 5%; equity $72 bn; debt $23 bn; tax rate 15%. I set the terminal growth to 5%, aligned with the overall industry growth. Discounting all the future FCFF and subtracting net debt, the fair value is calculated to be $191 per share, as detailed in the table below:

Downside Risks

  • CoreWeave’s top 2 customers accounted for around 77% of total revenue in FY24, with Microsoft (MSFT) being the largest at 62% of total revenue. As such, CoreWeave has significant customer concentration risk and their growth is largely decided by Microsoft’s cloud infrastructure expansion. However, as CoreWeave tries to expand their services to other Hyperscalers and enterprise customers, I’d anticipate the concentration risks will gradually diminish.
  • Due to CoreWeave’s substantial CAPEX spendings, I anticipate CoreWeave will need to raise debts or issue more equities in the near future. As discussed previously, the company plans to spend $20-$23 billion in CAPEX for FY25, a quite high level compared to its potential cash flow from operations. If CoreWeave raises more capital via stock offerings in the future, their stock price might face near-term pressure.
  • Lastly, to sustain its competitive advantages, CoreWeave needs to continue high spendings in data center expansions and GPU purchases. CoreWeave relies on Nvidia (NVDA) for key GPUs to its overall infrastructure build-out. As such, the relationship between Nvidia and CoreWeave could affect the availability of GPU products. In addition, the cloud Hyperscalers, like Microsoft, Amazon (AMZN) and Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL), might increase their own AI cloud infrastructure build-outs, which will increase competition in the AI cloud infrastructure market.

Conclusion

CoreWeave’s holistic AI infrastructure solution will benefit from the massive growth in AI training and AI inference workloads, in my view. I am initiating with a ‘Buy’ rating with a fair value of $191 per share.

data.title: Largest U.S. Bank Stocks Carry The Sector In May
data.description:
The US banks with the biggest market capitalizations outperformed the rest of the sector in May.

The median total return in May was 3.6% for the 212 banks in an S&P Global Market Intelligence analysis. This market performance was lifted by the 16 banks with a market cap greater than $10 billion as of May 30. The largest-cap group had a 7.8% median total return, ranging from 4.0% at First Citizens BancShares Inc. (FCNCA) to 11.0% at Citigroup Inc.

Less than 10% of the banks in the analysis had a negative monthly return. Archbold, Ohio-based Farmers & Merchants Bancorp Inc. (OTCQX:FMCB); Harrisburg, Pennsylvania-based Mid Penn Bancorp Inc. (MPB); and Hammond, Louisiana-based First Guaranty Bancshares Inc. (FGBI) were the worst market performers, trading down 9.0%, 7.6% and 5.9%, respectively. The only bank with a market cap greater than $2 billion that lost ground in the market was Flagstar Financial Inc. (FLG), which recorded a return of negative 1.6% in May after a 25.6% return through the first four months of the year.

Least expensive banks

Dallas-based First Foundation Inc. (FFWM) was the least expensive bank in the analysis by price-to-adjusted tangible book value (TBV) for the fifth consecutive month. Its valuation was 46.9% as of May 30 and would have been higher if preferred shares from a capital raise in July 2024 were converted to common stock. The conversion would have lowered the bank's basic TBV to $9.42 from $11.77 at March 31, according to an April 30 investor presentation.

First Foundation recorded net income of $6.9 million for the first quarter after posting net losses for the previous two quarters. The bank's net interest margin has risen for four consecutive quarters, and management is projecting continued expansion. With a return to profitability and a smaller balance sheet, First Foundation's capital ratios increased across the board on a quarter-over-quarter basis.

Three analysts, previously at Wedbush Securities, joined Jefferies this year and initiated coverage on a basket of US banks, including First Foundation at "buy." The analysts also started covering Bethesda, Maryland-based Eagle Bancorp Inc. (EGBN), ranked No. 2 by lowest price-to-adjusted TBV, with a "hold" rating and No. 18 Los Angeles-based Banc of California Inc. (BANC) with a "buy" recommendation.

Eagle Bancorp and First Internet Bancorp (INBK), which was the third-least expensive bank in the analysis, are two of the largest US banks that exceeded the 2006 commercial real estate regulatory guidance as of March 31.

Melville, New York-based First of Long Island Corp. (FLIC), which was the No. 8 bank by lowest valuation, will no longer be in the analysis as it was acquired by Englewood Cliffs, New Jersey-based ConnectOne Bancorp Inc. (CNOB) at the beginning of June.

Primis Financial Corp. (FRST), the 13th-cheapest bank, reported a $24.6 million gain in the first quarter from the deconsolidation of Panacea Financial Holdings. The McLean, Virginia-based bank's return on average tangible common equity jumped to 36.55% from 3.84% in the year-ago quarter.

In May, No. 15 Citigroup (C) announced the sale of its consumer banking business in Poland, as well as the sale of Citi Global Alternatives LLC. Additionally, Berkshire Hathaway Inc. disclosed that it exited its Citigroup stake of 14.6 million shares during the first quarter.

Most expensive banks

Abilene, Texas-based First Financial Bankshares Inc. (FFIN) ascended to the top valuation rank as of the end of May. Its price-to-adjusted TBV was 358.7%, up from 340.9% at April 30.

Dewitt, New York-based Community Financial System Inc. (CBU) slotted in at No. 2 following three consecutive months as the most expensive bank in the analysis.

The third-most expensive bank, Pathward Financial Inc. (CASH), received a noncompliance notice from Nasdaq Stock Market LLC for failing to file a Form 10-Q for the fiscal quarter ended March 31. Pathward's total return in May ran counter to the industry trend at negative 1.7%.

The No. 4 bank, The Bancorp Inc. (TBBK), announced that a deal to sell a problem real estate asset failed to close even after multiple deadline extensions.

On May 21, Oklahoma City-based BancFirst Corp. (BANF), the seventh-most expensive bank, announced its planned acquisition of Collinsville, Oklahoma-based American Bank of Oklahoma.

On May 29, No. 20 Wells Fargo & Co. (WFC) announced that it agreed to sell the assets of its rail equipment leasing business for $4.4 billion. On June 3, Wells Fargo (WFC) disclosed that the Federal Reserve removed the $1.95 trillion asset cap that was established for the bank.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Encouraging Q1 From Planet Labs Marks First Positive Operating Cash Flow
data.description:
Planet Labs' (NYSE:PL) share price has moved around quite a bit of late with earnings, and while it got appealingly low-priced when it dropped on Q4 earnings, I maintained my hold rating in March because the miss concerned me a little too. Now we have a Q1 earnings for the new fiscal year, and it's a lot more promising, but…

The price is up substantially Thursday on that estimate beat, which puts us back in the situation where, apart from Q4, Planet Labs has beat estimates every quarter for years. We were wondering if Q4 was a one-time issue, and these new earnings suggest it probably was, and the company is likely back on track.

Which means once again we're going to be looking over Planet Labs, seeing what's changed, what the numbers look like with the new figures, and the important accomplishment they beat on operating cash flow this quarter.

Q1: A Good Beat on Both EPS and Revenue

As mentioned, Planet Labs came in with a better than estimated earnings result, breaking even on a non-GAAP basis instead of the 3¢ loss they were anticipating. The beat was further encouraging because they also beat estimates on revenue by a little over $4 million. It doesn't sound like a lot, but Planet Labs is still a relatively small growth company, and growth that's a little bit faster can add up to a consequential improvement in the long run, especially with so many contracts of the business recurring. The most important, I think, is the gross margin.

That Planet Labs has been able to grow both its revenue and its gross margin year over year is an excellent trend, and reaching break-even, at least on a non-GAAP basis, is still a worthy milestone for a company that's still in the process of turning into a mature business.

That said, the estimate for the rest of the quarters of this fiscal year are for narrow non-GAAP losses, so we can't necessarily declare that threshold permanently crossed. Planet Labs' history of beating estimates though, and its improving margins, could mean they're getting closer and closer to the point where break-even is no longer a shock as it was this time.

Growing in Some Sectors

Not every sector of Planet Labs grew, however. In the conference call, they reported a slight year-over-year decline in the Civil Government sector, the result of a shorter contract happening to expire this quarter. They grew overall though, with the Defense and Intelligence sector showing the best results, up 20% year over year. That drove the overall growth, while the Commercial sector also stabilized after recent struggles.

Planet Labs is a relatively small company still, but they are in a lot of different market and government segments with their offerings. Government spending working the way it does, it may well continue to be the case that various political priorities favor some sectors over others at any given time, but Planet Labs appears to cover all such sectors pretty effectively, attracting new contracts here and there on all sorts of efforts.

Just recently, Planet Labs announced a new "7 figure" contract with the German Interior Ministry, and expanded a resource management agreement with the government of Wales. So while Civil Government seemed weak in Q1, such deals are being made and as they start to ramp up, it should mean that sector will perform well too.

As new, better satellites are put into orbit, Planet Labs can improve the quality of their offerings and potential open up new opportunities that weren't there before. Parsing all that data is complicated, and that's where AI comes in.

Planet Labs Remains Enthusiastic About AI

Last time we looked at Planet Labs, we talked a little about their partnership with Anthropic. That's still ongoing, but perhaps the more exciting thing is that they are working with Google AI on their Geospatial Reasoning effort.

A lot of the data Planet Labs' satellites necessarily collect in the course of their travel aren't used for much at present, only very specific images from very specific areas are often sold. Yet, that doesn't mean that data is ultimately worthless, as with AI parsing through it, noticing subtle changes in places that otherwise wouldn't be deserving of attention, the hope is that they'll be able to uncover even more insights, and Planet Labs, being the source of the data, could profit from what used to be unimportant side images.

Anthropic is a smaller, though relatively successful AI company, so it isn't necessarily a bad partner for Planet Labs to work with. Google is plainly much bigger in everything they do, though, so any relationship Planet Labs can have with them probably will directly lead to a more substantial result in pretty short order. It's not clear when this study by Google AI will lead to anything particularly accretive, but if Google is happy with Planet Labs' role in it, it could open up countless additional Commercial sector ventures.

I've always suspected Commercial would end up being the big sector for Planet Labs, but so far, they've defied my expectation with substantial government business growth. In the long run though, profitable Commercial deals could be even bigger, and Google is a step in that direction.

The Cash Flow First

Planet Labs is still not GAAP profitable, but that doesn't mean they're bleeding cash anymore, or heavily reliant on dilutive share offerings to keep the doors open. Q1 was the first time that they were operating cash flow positive, and relatively substantially so.

Q1 FY2025

Q1 FY2026

Operating FCF

($4.3 million)

Financing FCF

($2.4 million)

($9.2 million)

($0.3 million)

$5.7 million

(source: most recent 10-Q from SEC)

So operating cash flow is a huge positive, obviously, but the lack of that huge investing cash flow resulting from new share issues is no small matter either. The 2025 number includes some $43 million in sales of securities, while the 2026 number includes only $582,000.

Operating cash flow this past quarter was not only positive, it was around double the company's CapEx, and with the highest gross margin and more and more contracts being reported all the time, they may be able to remain at or near cash flow positive in the future.

Q2 and Other Pitfalls

So things are looking really great after Q1, but I don't want to give the impression that it's all sunshine and rainbows from here. Planet Labs still has some things to contend with, not the least of which being that they're probably not going to maintain break-even status next quarter.

During the conference call, Planet Labs reported that they expect substantially higher CapEx in Q2. That's not an increase in guidance on CapEx for the full year, just that more of it will be coming up in the second quarter, and subsequently will be a drag on next quarter's earnings.

They might overcome this timing if they continue growing the gross margin, but that's not so easy. They offered guidance on non-GAAP gross margin of 56%-57%, which was slightly down from the 59% they reported this quarter. The general trend toward improving margins is a good sign, but it's not a straight shot, and it might not end up as high as it was in the past quarter.

All of this means that break-even, even on a non-GAAP basis, is likely to remain the exception rather than the rule for the time being.

A Buy but Only at the Right Price

What does all that add up to? I'm liking Planet Labs' prospects a lot more after Q1, but I think we should also temper are expectations, especially after the price went up so much on Thursday. I'm going to upgrade it to a buy, but with the caveat that it's only a buy at the right price and if you've got a long-term view.

Planet Labs might well pull back some from the price it is right now, so being patient and looking for a better entry point could turn out to be a good move. Likewise, prices seem to be really sensitive to quarterly earnings of late, so you shouldn't buy unless you're comfortable holding for a few years, as you can't always count on estimate beats as a catalyst.

Investors should watch the revenue growth and the acquisition of new contracts, as they still need to grow from where they are right now to reach dependable profitability. They can do it, certainly, but there are a lot of unknowns right now in the process, as this is a relatively new sector. I'd watch margins too because the growth is a very good sign, but keep in mind, as mentioned above, they probably won't continue to grow margins every single quarter.

Q1 shows Planet Labs to be on the right track, and if you can get in for a reasonable price, I believe it's worth exploring at this point.

data.title: The Gabelli Gold Fund, Inc. Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
PORTFOLIO OBSERVATIONS

The gold price performed strongly in the first quarter of 2025, building on its gains over the past two years. Gold ended the quarter at $3,124 per ounce for a gain of about $500 per ounce or 19.0%. Gold mining equities returned in excess of 30%, outperforming the gold price by over fifty percent.

Over the past few years, the performance of the gold miners has underperformed the gold price. This was largely due to the cost of mining rising along with the gold price, thus negating the leverage gold miners earnings have to changes in the gold price. However, the sheer scale of the increase in the gold price means that gold mining companies' cash flows and profits should show a dramatic increase when first quarter earnings are released in the next few weeks. Investors are beginning to recognize this and gold equities, so far this year, have returned about twice the move in the gold price, nearer to the traditional relationship between gold and gold equities. This is positive, but at current gold equity valuations and assuming the gold price continues to appreciate, we believe gold equities have significant upside potential.

Until recently, the gold price has appreciated largely due to overseas central bank buying. However, more recently, investors have been adding to their gold holdings. This is evidenced by the rise in ounces of gold held by all the gold bullion ETFs. During the first quarter, gold ETFs added over 5m ounces to 87.9m ounces, which amounts to about $15bn. To put this in perspective, the high in ounces held in all ETFs was over 110m in 2020. Gold is increasingly being viewed by central banks as an alternative to holding short-dated U.S. and other government securities. Importantly, its attributes of being no one else's liability, liquid, and portable, are valuable in an increasingly volatile global economic and political environment. For private investors, gold can be a helpful diversification for portfolios.

TOP TEN SELECTED HOLDINGS*
Newmont Corp. (NEM) 7.7%
Kinross Gold Corp. (KGC) 6.1
Agnico Eagle Mines Ltd. (AEM) 5.9
Wheaton Precious Metals Corp. (WPM) 5.5
Northern Star Resources Ltd. (OTCPK:NESRF) 5.4
Alamos Gold Inc. (AGI) 4.8
Endeavour Mining plc (OTCQX:EDVMF) 4.1
Endeavour Mining plc 4.1
Lundin Gold Inc. (OTCQX:LUGDF) 3.5
Franco-Nevada Corp. (FNV) 3.5
Barrick Gold Corp. 3.2
*Percent of net assets as of March 31, 2025.

Unsurprisingly in a strong quarter for gold stocks, our larger holdings were the top contributors to performance. The biggest contributor was Agnico Eagle (5.9% of net assets as of March 31, 2025), our largest holding, which appreciated by 39.1% and added 3.5% to performance. Other leading contributors were Newmont (7.7%), Kinross (6.1%), and Alamos (4.8%). In terms of stock price performance, some of our smaller producers and development companies dominated. For example, Collective Mining (CNL, 0.4%), an explorer in Colombia, more than doubled, while producers G Mining Ventures (OTCPK:GMINF, 1.9%) and Discovery Silver (OTCQX:DSVSF, 0.4%) appreciated in excess of 70.0%. Other notable performers of positions in excess of 2.5% of the portfolio were AngloGold Ashanti (AU, 2.8%, +64.30%), Evolution Mining (OTCPK:CAHPF, 2.6%, +50.4%), and Lundin Gold (3.5%, +46.7%).

During the quarter, we reduced our exposure to royalty companies, which have no cost of production, and to some of our recent winners. The proceeds were used to establish new positions in a number of smaller producers that are, in many cases, commissioning new mines and offer greater upside potential than the larger and more highly valued gold producers. To effectuate this, we sold Osisko Gold Royalties (OR), Gold Road Resources (OTCPK:ELKMF), and RTG Mining (OTCPK:RTGGF), and reduced holdings such as Agnico Eagle (5.9%), Lundin Gold (3.5%), and Wesdome (OTCQX:WDOFF, 2.5%). New positions included Aris Mining (ARMN, 0.2%), Calibre Mining (OTCQX:CXBMF, 1.1%), Discovery Silver (0.4%), Equinox Gold (EQX, 0.6%), Fresnillo (OTCPK:FNLPF, 1.1%), IAMGold (IAG, 0.9%), Montage Gold (OTCQX:MAUTF, 0.8%), and Southern Cross Gold (OTCPK:MWSNF, 0.1%).

It seems that many of the arrangements that have governed the global economy are now being questioned or are, at a minimum, under threat. The most obvious of these include trade flows and geopolitical agreements and could easily expand to the world's financial architecture, such as the role of the dollar and capital flows. In this environment, gold should perform well and gold equities, which are over twenty five percent lower than their 2011 high, offer an opportunity for significant capital gains and income.

LET'S TALK STOCKS

Kinross Gold Corp. (6.1%) (KGC – $12.61 – NYSE), headquartered in Toronto, Canada, produces approximately 2 million ounces of gold per year. The company is generating significant amounts of free cash flow, which it is using to pay down debt and pay a dividend. We expect the company to begin buying back its stock in the second half of the year. We believe that Kinross's Great Bear growth project in northern Ontario will be one of the largest and lowest cost mines in Canada once production begins in 2029.

Alamos Gold (4.8%) (AGI – $26.72 – NYSE) owns two mines in Ontario and one mine in Mexico. The company recently completed the acquisition of a mine which is adjacent to its low cost Island Gold Mine in northern Ontario. The acquisition provides for meaningful synergies between the two operations. Once fully integrated, we expect the combined Island complex to be one the largest, lowest cost, and longest life mines in Canada.

Artemis Gold (2.5%) (ARTG - $11.89/17.11 CAD - CVE) is completing construction of what will be one of the largest and lowest cost mines in Canada. The Blackwater mine in British Columbia poured its first gold in January, and is now in the process of ramping up to full production levels. Once at full capacity, the company will be in a good position to grow production further through a mill expansion. We expect that Artemis will eventually generate meaningful amounts of free cash flow, which it will distribute to shareholders in the form of a dividend.

data.title: A Major Market Reversal Is Likely Coming
data.description:
It has been a pretty volatile six months for markets, with both the S&P 500 (SPY) and the Nasdaq (QQQ) experiencing a sharp sell-off into right around bear market territory between the end of February and early April over fears of Trump's trade war. However, since then, the market has experienced a V-shaped recovery, as Trump initiated a pause on all trade parties except China and then later reached a temporary trade truce with China.

As such, it appears the market is pricing in bullishness on the Trump administration hammering out trade deals with the vast majority of U.S. trade partners in the near future, as well as expecting continued strength from the AI boom along with Trump's aggressive deregulatory and anti-tax agenda.

That being said, I am not a believer in the market's optimism, and I expect a major market reversal to occur in the near future. In this article, I will detail several reasons why and share how I am positioning my portfolio right now in light of this uncertainty.

Why a Major Market Reversal is Likely Coming

The biggest reason why I am not optimistic on the market right now is that it is significantly overvalued based on virtually every valuation model out there. This includes the Buffett indicator model, the price-to-earnings model, the price-to-sales model, the interest rate model, and the S&P 500 mean reversion model—all of which indicate that the market is between overvalued and strongly overvalued right now. As such, I see very unfavorable risk-reward simply on a normalized level for the market. However, I also see a lot of uncertainties and potential headwinds that make this not even a normalized environment, and therefore I would require the market to appear meaningfully undervalued right now relative to its historical averages to consider investing in the S&P 500 at the moment.

So, what are these uncertainties and potential headwinds? Well, the biggest one is the ongoing tensions with China. Sure, there was a temporary trade truce, but since then, both countries have accused the other of violating the terms of that agreement and have, in turn, embarked on a tit-for-tat confrontation—with China restricting the flow of rare-earth minerals to the United States, while the U.S., in turn, has begun suspending visas to Chinese students as well as restricting the export of ethane through companies like Energy Transfer (ET) and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) to China. As such, they are both trying to force the other to come to the bargaining table.

While Trump and Xi did reportedly have a constructive phone call today, and there will likely be a meeting in the near future to resume trade talks, it is important to keep in mind that these are two adversaries that are increasingly finding their interests in opposition to each other rather than aligned. There is also a growing possibility of a war between them over Taiwan and geopolitical dominance in the Asia-Pacific region at large—and eventually probably the entire world—in the not-too-distant future. As such, it is hard for me to get too optimistic about a true and lasting treaty being reached on trade between the U.S. and China, as any agreement is likely going to either be very limited in scope or not honored to the degree that it should be.

As such, I think the market is being way too optimistic about the potential outcome of U.S.-China trade negotiations. Given the significant amount of trade that is done between the two countries and the ramifications of trade falling precipitously between them on the global economy—including several leading components of the S&P 500—this is a major potential headwind to the market that I do not think is being properly priced in.

On top of that, the rest of Trump's trade war with its other trading partners is far from over, too. Sure, there is a pause, but that 90-day pause is quickly running out of runway, as in early July it will be over. Yet, so far, only a trade agreement with the United Kingdom has been rolled out, and there appear to remain significant differences between the U.S. and Japan. The recent election in South Korea of a more China-friendly president could potentially put an agreement with South Korea in jeopardy as well. Meanwhile, the newly elected Prime Minister of Canada appears intent on toeing a firm line with Trump, and the EU and the U.S.—while apparently engaged in urgent negotiations—appear to be far apart from reaching a new agreement as well.

Therefore, the market seems to be far too optimistic about the outcome of these agreements too. Sure, it is certainly possible that all of these major trading partners of the United States come to the table and can hammer out a mutually agreeable deal that results in similar or even greater levels of trade than previously was conducted between them. However, it is also entirely possible that one, several, or even all of them fail to come to terms with the United States. Additionally, the Trump administration has made clear that they intend on slapping a baseline 10% tariff on all trade partners even in a best-case scenario, which means that there will likely be additional frictional loss on trade relative to what there was before these tariffs were put in place—at the very least on the U.S. consumer side.

In addition to trade and geopolitical tensions, there is another major risk facing the markets that again they seem all too passive about—namely, the U.S. fiscal situation and the effect it is having on interest rates. With the so-called "big beautiful bill" that the Trump administration has been pushing, the deficit is likely going to continue to grow, leading to a major increase in U.S. debt in the coming years. This will put increasing pressure on the bond markets, not only because of growing uncertainty about the U.S.'s fiscal solvency, but simply because it is going to lead to so much more demand for bond investors, even as many global central banks and investors are increasingly diversifying away from the dollar.

This concern is being signaled loud and clear by the strong recent moves in gold and silver prices, as it reflects growing demand from central banks and other investors for alternative stores of wealth to the U.S. dollar. On top of that, the potential—at least short-term—inflation uptick from tariffs, as well as these runaway deficits, are making it hard for the Fed to justify near-term interest rate cuts, which are also putting further upward pressure on bond markets.

On top of that, Elon Musk and several other Republicans have recently spearheaded a pushback against the so-called big beautiful bill, and if their protest succeeds—sure, it may ultimately improve the U.S.'s fiscal outlook moving forward—but in the near term, it could lead to the Trump tax cuts from his first term at least temporarily expiring, leading to a significant drag on corporate earnings, which in turn would likely pose a major headwind to the stock market. As a result, no matter how you cut it, it seems that the most likely outcome from the current U.S. budget debate will likely pose—at least at some point—a headwind to the market.

Investor Takeaway

So how am I investing? While it may sound like I am a doom-and-gloomer who thinks that you should put 100% of your money into gold (GLD) and silver (SLV), buy an underground bunker, and hide, I actually believe that the current situation simply makes being well diversified more important than ever. I am not encouraging investors to completely abandon broader equity markets and positions in more aggressive sectors. Ultimately, I think it is very hard to consistently accurately forecast macro events, especially in the near term, and the market is famously unpredictable in its behavior in the short term. Therefore, I am not saying you should go all in on one direction or the other. However, I do think it is important to put a greater weighting in one's portfolio on the more defensive and inflation-resistant sectors of the economy.

At the same time, you can still maintain strong upside potential by investing on a value basis, as well as leaning into macro tailwinds like the AI boom, reshoring of industrial activity to the United States, and also focusing on those markets within the United States that are most business-friendly and therefore likely to attract the most investment from companies bringing their production to the United States. In particular, I like the energy infrastructure sector (UTF), with some of my favorites being midstream businesses (AMLP) as well as diversified infrastructure plays like Brookfield Infrastructure Partners (BIP)(BIPC) and Brookfield Renewable Partners (BEP)(BEPC). These should all benefit from the AI boom and reshoring of industrial activity to the United States, while also being quite stagflation resistant due to their highly contracted nature and the inflation indexation of their contracts.

I also like strategically located REITs (VNQ) in the residential, industrial, and land sectors as these should benefit from the reshoring trend while still being pretty stagflation resistant. In particular, I like Rayonier (RYN) because it owns a large amount of land in Sunbelt markets and is taking active steps to capitalize on higher-and-best-use opportunities in those markets while at the same time trading at about a 30% discount to its net asset value and having very little debt on its balance sheet.

Finally, I also think it makes sense to own a lot of precious metals right now—both in terms of the actual bullion itself as well as undervalued mining stocks (GDX) that can provide material upside even if gold simply hovers around its current range instead of needing to move materially higher from its already elevated levels. This is because precious metals should outperform in scenarios in which geopolitical risks manifest themselves and during periods of stagflation.

This is the approach we are taking as we continue to deploy capital into our well-diversified portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: June's Best Blue-Chip Fat Pitches: Opportunities Hiding In Plain Sight
data.description:
A lot of people want to know what I consider the best Buffet-style "Fat pitches" blue-chip bargains.

Wait for a fat pitch and then swing for the fences." Warren Buffett

These are the companies that possess blue-chip quality or higher, characterized by strong balance sheets and excellent management teams. They're not just wonderful companies at fair prices, but wonderful companies at wonderful prices.

It's far better to buy a wonderful company at a fair price than a fair company at a wonderful price." Warren Buffett

Each month, Dividend Kings analysts (we're bringing on a new analyst in the coming months) offer their favorite blue-chip opportunities for that month.

Let me share my new approach to recommending the best blue-chip "fat pitches," based on what I own in the ZEUS Legacy Charity Hedge Fund.

My family's portfolio has been performing exceptionally well compared to hedge funds (HFND is the Vanguard of hedge funds) and the traditional 60-40 stock-bond portfolio.

Since 2016, I've recommended nearly 600 companies more than 3,000 times, with 66% of those recommendations increasing (excluding dividends, which average a 5% yield) within the first year, resulting in an average increase of 10.2% (or approximately 15.2% including dividends).

US Stocks Since June 2016 (When I Started Recommending Stocks On Seeking Alpha).

During that time, U.S. stocks have seen a 14% compound annual growth rate, with value stocks increasing by 11% and high-yield stocks by 10%.

In other words, value stocks have achieved their historical returns (approximately 11%) as have high-yield stocks.

The market has done better thanks to big tech fueling significant gains for 30% of the index.

What's my secret? What crystal ball do I use to beat high-yield stocks' annual returns by 50% over almost a decade?

Why Fundamentals Are All You Need

I don't use technical analysis, trying to time tops or bottoms for blue-chip stocks.

Why not?

Time Frame (Years) Total Returns Explained By Fundamentals/Valuations
1 Day 0.02%
1 month 0.39%
3 month 1.2%
6 months 3.5%
1 7%- 12-month stock price targets!
2 14%
3 21%
4 28%
5 29%
6 54%
7 63%
8 72%
9 81%
10+ 90%
20+ 91%
30+ 97% Retirement time period!

(Sources: JPMorgan, Bank of America, Fidelity, RIA, Princeton)

Within 12 months, 93% of stock returns are driven not by fundamentals, such as dividends or earnings growth, but by sentiment, momentum, and luck.

  • This is why I don't do price targets but "fundamentally justified total return potentials."

Why not use technical analysis or analyst 12-month price targets? Because those estimates are based on an analyst estimating fundamentals (like EPS) and then guessing the PE multiple!

To be right in the short term, an analyst must be accurate about the fundamentals (earnings) and the sentiment (as reflected in the PE ratio that the market assigns to a stock).

However, remember that over 12 months (the traditional price target timeframe), 93% of stock returns are driven by chance.

So, in the short term, luck is 13X as powerful as fundamentals.

Even if you can nail earnings exactly, it's a crap shoot whether the market will respond to those earnings as you expect.

But over the long term? Fundamentals are 33X as powerful as luck.

And that's why I disregard technical analysis and focus solely on the best fundamental data.

How I Value Stocks

Remember that predicting the future is impossible. All we can do is make directional bets and try to be as close as possible with our models.

All models are wrong, but some are useful." George E.P Box (UK Statistician)

That's why I use the best available sources, including the FactSet consensus (92% accuracy over 20 years, across all companies).

The reason for using the FactSet consensus is simple. Why reinvent the wheel? If any individual model is wrong to some extent, and all models (bottom-up FactSet consensus) are 92% accurate, then go with the consensus where the models' "wrongness" mostly cancels each other out.

  • A 92% accuracy rate means that a 10% estimate corresponds to a 9.2% to 10.8% actual EPS growth range.

What about valuations?

Let The Market Tell You What A Stock Is Worth

Let's consider Realty Income (O) as an example. What's Realty's discounted cash flow fair value? I don't know and I don't care.

Let's consider Realty Income (O) as an example. What is Realty's discounted cash flow fair value? I don't know and I don't care. The discount rate is unknowable until after the fact.

What about the correct cap rate? I don't know, I don't care.

What I care about is the multiple that professional money managers (who control 80% of fund flows into and out of stocks) assign to that specific stock, in the case of REITs, the FFO multiple.

  • REIT equivalent of EPS.

And what's the right FFO multiple? Anything other than an objectively market-determined multiple would be speculation.

  • "Realty's FFO multiple is going to be X, higher than its historical norm because of X, Y, or Z" requires me to be right about the future.

Why guess about a potential future when we have years (or decades) of historical data confirming what millions (or even billions) of investors have paid for a stock over years or decades?

Remember that value is "what others are willing to pay you."

Who is "others?" Other investors.

I need to know what other investors consider "fair value," and here's why.

Yield (today's income) + growth (cash flow, dividend growth) = total returns over time.

If you buy at fair value, you can expect that exact return.

If you buy below fair value, you receive a long-term valuation boost. If you pay more than fair value, you incur a valuation drag that reduces your returns.

For Realty Income, a stable blue-chip with a stable growth rate (management guidance 4% to 6% long-term), the company's objective market-determined fair value is around 18X to 19X FFO, with a 10-year average of 18.7X.

That's an objective fact. There's no doubt about that. My value model assumes that Realty Income if it continues to grow at historical rates (as management and analysts expect), will continue to "mean revert" around a mean of 19 times cash flow.

Realty Income's 20-year FFO range is 17X to 19X FFO (including with interest rates as high as 7%).

In other words, by examining the 10- to 20-year historical average multiple ranges, we can factor in any business conditions or factors that have prevailed over that time.

As Ben Graham explained, "Over the long term, the market is a weighing machine that correctly weighs the substance of a company."

A period of 10-plus years is statistically 90% or more significant, or in other words, over this period, random noise, luck, momentum, freakouts, booms, busts, bear and bull markets, and even bubbles are canceled out.

For example, a stable business, such as a utility, with over 30 years of history, can be estimated with 97% statistical confidence regarding its fair value price-to-earnings - PE - ratio.

A company that has grown at X% for 30 years and is likely to continue growing at the same rate in the future is worth its long-term average (or median) price-to-earnings - PE - ratio, for example.

And once you know a company's likely growth rate, and its historical market-determined long-term fair value multiple? Then all you need to know is if that dividend yield is safe.

A stock that cuts its dividend is a broken thesis. Not always, but historically, dividend cutters are terrible investors, achieving inflation-adjusted losses of 91% over the last 50 years.

Remember that investing is all about probabilities. There are no certainties—only probability curves based on the best available data, which are always subject to change as new facts emerge.

But if you follow the fundamentals? Based on the best available data? And let the market tell you a company's fair value? Then, disciplined financial science, as I practice, a 100% rules-based approach to fundamentals-driven investing, can result in significant gains.

Such as beating high-yield investors by 50% (5% per year) for nine years. Without once having to guess what will happen in the next few months, quarters, or even years.

My Top 5 Buy List For June: A New Approach

I currently own 10 stocks, plus hedges in the form of managed futures (CTA).

Core holdings and opportunistic "Buffett-style fat pitches."

With less time for articles (due to business and family responsibilities), I can only offer my most assertive and confident recommendations, those fat pitches that I'm willing to buy.

Does that mean that I only recommend what I own? No, that wouldn't be tenable.

  • I once tried that, buying $100 worth of every stock I recommended, and ended up owning 200 companies (which is way too much time to track in the portfolio software).

But for my Top 5 List recs? I can only use companies that I own.

But how do I select the top 5? Given that the Top Buy list is updated monthly, I imagine that most people want to know, "What do you think is the most explosive 'Buffett style fat pitch?"

In other words, over the next year, what coiled springs might deliver Buffett-like returns from blue-chip bargains hiding in plain sight?

What I did was use the Dividend Kings Zen Research Terminal to look at all the companies I own.

Equal Weighted Version Of ZEUS (No Hedges)

An equally weighted version of ZEUS would be 10% into each company which yields 4.1%, 35% historically undervaluled, with Ultra-SWAN (12.8/13) quality, with 87% S&P long-term risk management percentile (top 13% of all global companies) with 12.3% CAGR growth and 16.3% CAGR long-term return potential but almost 21% CAGR (Buffett-like) returns over the next five years.

Historical Returns Since 2000

However, over time, my actual holdings might increase from 10 to 20, 30, or even a Berkshire, such as 37 (Berkshire has owned as many as 63 companies over the decades).

For the top 5, I'm selecting companies with the top 12-month return potential, adjusted for the most likely ones to return to their historical fair value.

  • More challenged stocks, like UnitedHealth (UNH) or Humana (HUM), are not likely to return to fair value within a year (which is why they didn't make the list).

My Top 5 For June

Here they are, AES (AES), Alexandria Real Estate (ARE), Amazon (AMZN), Nvidia (NVDA), and Realty Income (O).

My top five for June offer a 49% historical discount, which means a 100% upside to fair value.

  • A 15% CAGR value boost over five years, assuming they all return to fair value during that time.

1- and 5-Year Consensus Fundamentally Total Return Potential

  • Not a forecast.
  • If and only if a company grows as expected today, and returns to historical fair value, then this is the return you'd make, including dividends.

AES Corp (AES): 255% 5-Year Return Potential 25.5% CAGR

Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) 236% 5-Year Total Return Potential = 24.3% CAGR

Amazon (AMZN) 268% 5-Year Return Potential = 26.4% CAGR

Nvidia (NVDA): 238% 5-Year Return Potential = 23.6% CAGR

Realty Income (O) 118% 5-Year Return Potential = 15% CAGR

What Might Go Wrong

When the facts change I change my mind." John Maynard Keynes

"In this business, if you're right six out of ten times you're very good." Peter Lynch

Guess what happens from time to time with fundamentals? They can go down... a lot.

I recommended Albermarle (ALB) when the price collapsed and the fundamentals were supposed to turn around within a year or two.

That turnaround never materialized.

Lithium prices continued to fall, and the fundamentals remained in decline.

Guess what the total return potential looks like now?

By the end of 2027, 11% CAGR.

Is the company going bankrupt? Probably not.

Will the dividend be cut? Probably not.

However, a return to fair value by the end of 2027 no longer offers attractive return potential, as the fundamental outlook continues to deteriorate.

Polaris (PII) is a blue-chip whose fundamentals have been challenging, making it not only speculative but also now with negative return potential through the end of 2027.

And that includes a 6.5% dividend yield.

35% of my recommendations (excluding dividends) from the last nine years have not generated a profit in the first year.

The 10.2% average capital gain (ignoring dividends) includes 35% of stocks that don't go up in the first year. However, individual stocks can decline and continue to decline.

Since 1980, 44% of all stocks have fallen 70% or more and never recovered. The median peak decline? 89%

This is what single-stock risk looks like.

Stocks are a "risk asset" because there is a real risk of losing money, potentially permanently.

That's why you own a diversified portfolio and never invest in a single company, regardless of how impressive its fundamentals appear today.

In a year? Two years? Five years? The fundamentals will look different. We don't know in what direction or to what extent.

Bottom Line: June Offers Incredible Buffett-Style Return Potential

Next month, I'll have a new top five, but this month's top 5 offers an incredible 49% historical discount, a 4% very low-risk yield today, and over 100% return potential in 2025.

  • A full return to fair value within 12 months is unusual and not expected.
  • If it does happen, it will be accompanied by returns of over 100%, justified by the fundamentals as we understand them today.

However, this is an example of how disciplined financial science can uncover incredible opportunities in any market. Including today's 15% overvalued S&P.
